posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked:
@Steve_McL hosted @Mike_Evans and I last night to help Mike taste through some samples of a potential new Berserker Day winery (more on that later). We gilded the lily with a couple of young White Burgundies (apologies to the potential Berserker Day wines that followed).

Two brilliant wines in their own way. Do they need more time? Of course. But it’s good to get a read at a young age. Guffens Heynen!

  • 2020 Guffens-Heynen Mâcon-Pierreclos Tri de Chavigne - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Mâcon-Pierreclos (12/13/2023)
    Blind for others. Slight reduction. Matchstick on the palate more than nose. Rich- rounder than the other white on the table- A powerhouse- real Chardonnay with backbone and an oily, rich palate that lingers on. Really pops with air. Great wine that on this night outshone the baby Batard on the table.
  • 2020 Château de la Maltroye Bâtard-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Bâtard-Montrachet Grand Cru (12/13/2023)
    Steve and I had similar notes- Really tight and unyielding at first. I thought it was something quirky like a Gouges NSG blanc. With airtime and a decant, this rounded out and improved, more finesse, yielding more light fruit, a good bit of spice, mint, coconut. A lot of power in reserve. 13.5% Alc. OK now, but clearly this just needs to sleep for 5 or 10 years.

IMG_15311920×2875 333 KB

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
In the name of science, we uncorked a Batard baby…a mistake surely, but SOMEONE has to do this thankless but vitally important wine work. You’re welcome.

The Guffens-Heynen Chassange look-alike from Macon was delicious. More open, balanced and preferred around the table. Great stuff. Run, do not walk…

Like it or not, I think we’ve all internalized the 3-level Burgundy hierarchy of village, 1er and grand cru to some degree and we carry a partly subjective bias; its a fun to be reminded by blind tasting how good a well-made wine can be, regardless of label.

(Note to self: don’t go up against @Dennis_Atick in a blinded comparison… )

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Once you see the vineyards, it makes sense… Le Chavigne is mid-slope, nothing but stones, very old vines for the most part. And then the farming: minimal cultivation (works better with Chardonnay than Pinot Noir), very late hedging, no leaf pulling… And that is before the winemaking. Happily also under Diam 30 so no need for a very high dose of free sulfites that makes the wine closed until it oxidizes.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Dennis_Atick:

Guffens Heynen!

I’m sensing that like viking war-cry!

Some wines just over-deliver for the price. I had a Robert-Denogent Les Cras 2017 that just knocked me on my arse a while back. I would have put this bottle against many GC Burgs. Gotta love that. It’s those bottles that remind you of the thrill of the hunt.

Cheers!
